purpose: GECScore grammar-error-density signal (M1, stdlib, model-free): how little a grammar-error corrector changes a text. Reports gecscore = the difflib Gestalt character-similarity ratio (autojunk off; 2*M/(len(s)+len(corrected)), NOT a max(len)- normalized edit distance) between the text and its grammar-corrected form, in [0,1], plus the raw correction-span count (gec_n_corrections). The grammar corrector is the only load-bearing model dependency, so it is the M1/M2 seam: M1 runs over an INJECTED corrector (StubGecBackend, no model); M2 swaps in LanguageTool (Java on PATH) or GECToR (torch) behind the same correct(text)->str seam. In the literature AI text tends to HIGHER gecscore (near-zero error density) than human text (GECScore, arXiv:2405.04286; the paper's claimed 98.62% avg AUROC across XSum + WritingPrompts is VERIFIED from the primary source but is the paper's own claim, NOT reproduced on SETEC's corpus — a LEAD, not a prior asserted here), an axis orthogonal to the probability (Binoculars/surprisal/curvature/spectral) and distributional (glass-box stylometry) surfaces. The detection direction is PINNED as GEC_AI_DIRECTION = "gt" (higher gecscore = the AI-like direction) and asserted in a test — silent sign inversion is the detection family's shared failure mode. Descriptive only: emits VALUES + a PROVISIONAL band over the value's OWN axis (indeterminate / low_error_density / high_error_density) carrying calibration_status heuristic + calibration_anchor user-baseline-required. There is NO is_ai / is_human / label / verdict / decision key; the claim-license refuses any authorship verdict. CRITICAL: gecscore INVERTS on ESL/dialect prose (a polished non-native author scores near 1.0, the AI direction), so the surface co-emits a fairness_dialect_guardrails caution block (results.fairness_guardrails) and names the inversion first-class in the claim-license — it is gated behind fairness_dialect_guardrails per the ROADMAP.
use_when: You want a grammar-error-density discrimination axis orthogonal to the probability and distributional surfaces
You're assembling a multi-signal evidence pack and want a GECScore reading
Target is >= 50 words (below the floor the normalized edit distance is noisy)
do_not_use_when: You want an AI/human verdict (this surface ships uncalibrated and refuses one)
The prose may be ESL / non-native / non-standard-dialect (gecscore INVERTS — read the co-emitted fairness_dialect_guardrails block first)
You need robustness to adversarial error injection (deliberate typos defeat gecscore)
You need values comparable across GEC backends (M1 stub != M2 LanguageTool/GECToR)

artifacts: stdout markdown report (default), --json envelope, --batch JSONL/JSON rows, or --out PATH

compute.cost_note: stdlib only at M1 (difflib char-level similarity over an injected corrector; no model). The M2 real GEC backends are the model-CPU seam — LanguageTool needs Java on PATH; GECToR needs torch. Neither is loaded at M1.

examples.description: GECScore over a single target (JSON envelope). With no real grammar corrector wired (M1 default), the CLI abstains (available:false / missing_dependency) rather than report a stub identity gecscore=1.0; a real M2 backend is required to report a score.
GECScore with a declared ESL background (co-emits the fairness gate)
Batch feature-column extraction over a manifest (the M2 corpus-run shape)

references: GECScore — Detecting Machine-Generated Texts by Multi-Population Aware Optimization for Grammatical Error Correction, arXiv:2405.04286 (2024 preprint; the 98.62% avg AUROC claim is confirmed from the primary source but [UNVERIFIED on SETEC's corpus]): https://arxiv.org/abs/2405.04286
